narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds. The model estimated a 77% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and resources. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance in school, particularly your low rankings relative to peers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance was your Year 1 grade decile, also in the bottom 10% compared to the average around the 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about consistent early academic struggles. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.10 helped somewhat by pulling the prediction slightly toward passing—since it's not drastically out of line with some successful students' profiles averaging 3.21—it wasn't strong enough to offset the other red flags. Your LSAT score of 30, well below the dataset average of about 37, and middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger high school deciles and higher test scores, your profile signals higher risk to the model, even accounting for the full-time study path most students (over 90%) pursue. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model prioritizes these metrics for bar passage odds. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and perhaps gaining legal experience via internships to build a more competitive profile. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value resilience like yours. Keep pushing; your determination as a Black man navigating these challenges positions you well for the long game in law.
